perturbation.original_text: Any controversy between the parties arising under this Agreement and not resolved by the parties shall be determined in accordance with the laws of the State of Michigan.
perturbation.changed_text: Any controversy between the parties arising under this Agreement and not resolved by the parties shall be addressed using principles derived from the laws of the State of Michigan.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'determined in accordance with the laws' to 'addressed using principles derived from the laws' introduces ambiguity. It's unclear what 'principles' are applicable, and whether those principles are actually consistent with Michigan law. The ambiguity weakens the enforcement of legal standards because it becomes subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCL § 600.601 establishes the jurisdiction of Michigan courts. By stating that controversies will be 'addressed using principles derived from' Michigan law, instead of being 'determined in accordance with' Michigan law, the modified text suggests that the decision might not fully adhere to the established laws and legal precedents of Michigan, potentially undermining the court's proper jurisdiction and application of established law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 600.601 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 600.601 Download Section Chapter 600 Act 236 of 1961 236-1961-6 Previous Section Next Section REVISED JUDICATURE ACT OF 1961 (EXCERPT) Act 236 of 1961 600.601 Circuit court; jurisdiction and power. Sec. 601. (1) The circuit court has the power and jurisdiction that is any of the following: (a) Possessed by courts of record at the common law, as altered by the state constitution of 1963, the laws of this state, and the rules of the supreme court. (b) Possessed by courts and judges in chancery in England on March 1, 1847, as altered by the state constitution of 1963, the laws of this state, and the rules of the supreme court. (c) Prescribed by the rules of the supreme court. (2) The circuit court has exclusive jurisdiction over condemnation cases commenced under the drain code of 1956, 1956 PA 40, MCL 280.1 to 280.630. (3) In a judicial circuit in which the circuit court is affected by a plan of concurrent jurisdiction adopted under chapter 4, the circuit court has concurrent jurisdiction with the probate court or the district court, or both, as provided in the plan of concurrent jurisdiction. (4) The family division of circuit court has jurisdiction as provided in chapter 10. History: 1961, Act 236, Eff. Jan. 1, 1963 ;-- Am. 1996, Act 388 , Eff. Jan. 1, 1998 ;-- Am. 2002, Act 678 , Eff. Apr. 1, 2003 ;-- Am. 2005, Act 326 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 27, 2005 ;-- Am. 2012, Act 338 , Eff. Jan. 1, 2013
perturbation.justification: The modification in the contract text, changing 'determined in accordance with the laws' to 'addressed using principles derived from the laws,' introduces ambiguity regarding the legal standard to be applied in resolving disputes. This weakens the enforceability of Michigan law. The original text ensures controversies are resolved under the established laws of Michigan, whereas the changed text uses principles, which could be interpreted subjectively and may not fully align with Michigan law. The Law Explanation supports that the modified text suggests that the decision might not fully adhere to the established laws and legal precedents of Michigan. Scraped Law Snippet 1, MCL § 600.601, establishes the jurisdiction of Michigan courts. By using the term 'principles derived from,' the agreement potentially undermines the court's proper jurisdiction and application of established law, creating a legal ambiguity and contradiction, which fits the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall incorporate and in all respects be subject to the "General Terms and Conditions" and the applicable Rate Schedule (as stated above) set forth in Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff, Third Revised Volume No. 1, as may be revised from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement should generally incorporate the "General Terms and Conditions" and the applicable Rate Schedule (as stated above) set forth in Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff, Third Revised Volume No. 1, as may be revised from time to time.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall incorporate and in all respects be subject to' with 'should generally incorporate' weakens the binding nature of the FERC Gas Tariff. It creates uncertainty about which specific terms and conditions apply. The word 'generally' creates a loophole that allows parties to selectively adhere to the tariff, making it ambiguous and difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 CFR § 154.1 requires tariffs to be clear, definite, and readily accessible. By stating that the agreement 'should generally incorporate' the tariff, it introduces ambiguity about the exact terms that are included, making the tariff terms less definite and accessible and potentially undermining the regulatory oversight mandated by FERC.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 CFR § 154.1 - Application; Obligation to file. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 154.1 - Application; Obligation to file. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 154.1 Application; Obligation to file. (a) The provisions of this part apply to filings pursuant to section 4 of the Natural Gas Act . (b) Every natural gas company must file with the Commission and post in conformity with the requirements of this part, schedules showing all rates and charges for any transportation or sale of natural gas subject to the jurisdiction of the Commission , and the classifications, practices, rules, and regulations affecting such rates, charges, and services, together with all contracts related thereto. (c) No natural gas company may file, under this part, any new or changed rate schedule or contract for the performance of any service for which a certificate of public convenience and necessity or certificate amendment must be obtained pursuant to section 7(c) of the Natural Gas Act , until such certificate has been issued. (d) For the purposes of paragraph (b) of this section, any contract that conforms to the form of service agreement that is part of the pipeline's tariff pursuant to § 154.110 does not have to be filed. Any contract or executed service agreement which deviates in any material aspect from the form of service agreement in the tariff is subject to the filing requirements of this part. Natural Gas Act CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original binding agreement to incorporate the FERC Gas Tariff by changing "shall incorporate and in all respects be subject to" to "should generally incorporate". This introduces ambiguity, making the tariff terms less definite. According to the Law Explanation, 18 CFR § 154.1 requires tariffs to be clear and definite. The changed text undermines this requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 18 CFR § 154.1, states that natural gas companies must file schedules showing all rates, charges, rules and regulations, together with all contracts related thereto, implying a necessity for clarity and adherence, which the modified text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: All notices and communications with respect to this Agreement shall be in writing by mail, e-mail, or fax, or other means as agreed to by the parties, and sent to the addresses stated below or to any other such address(es) as may be designated in writing by mail, e-mail, or fax, or other means similarly agreed to:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices and communications with respect to this Agreement may be in writing by mail, e-mail, or fax, or other means as deemed suitable by the initiating party, and directed to the addresses stated below or other addresses as casually indicated.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be' to 'may be' makes written notice optional. Replacing 'as agreed to by the parties' with 'as deemed suitable by the initiating party' gives unilateral control over communication methods, which may violate due process requirements and creates uncertainty. Also 'casually indicated' weakens the reliability of addresses.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 717c(a) requires that natural gas companies maintain just and reasonable practices. Making written notice optional and allowing unilateral control over communication methods can lead to unjust practices as it can make it more difficult for one party to prove that they notified the other party.

Rates and charges (a) Just and reasonable rates and charges All rates and charges made, demanded, or received by any natural-gas company for or in connection with the transportation or sale of natural gas subject to the jurisdiction of the Commission, and all rules and regulations affecting or pertaining to such rates or charges, shall be just and reasonable, and any such rate or charge that is not just and reasonable is declared to be unlawful. (b) Undue preferences and unreasonab
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguities that contradict 15 U.S.C. § 717c(a), which requires just and reasonable practices for natural gas companies. The original text requires notices to be sent to agreed-upon addresses, ensuring both parties acknowledge and agree to the methods of communication. The changed text makes written notice optional ('may be' instead of 'shall be') and allows one party to unilaterally decide the communication method ('as deemed suitable by the initiating party') and weakens the reliability of addresses ('casually indicated'). This undermines the 'just and reasonable' standard of 15 U.S.C. § 717c(a), as it creates uncertainty and potential for one party to disadvantage the other through arbitrary communication practices. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states, 'all rules and regulations affecting or pertaining to such rates or charges, shall be just and reasonable, and any such rate or charge that is not just and reasonable is declared to be unlawful.' The revised contract language could lead to unjust practices, contradicting the statute.
